common_name: Ring of Fire Cayenne Pepper (AAS Early Compact Cayenne)

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; Ring of Fire is an All-America Selections award-winning cayenne pepper developed by Park Seed — earlier-maturing and more compact than standard Long Red Cayenne, with upward-pointing fruits that ripen earlier (60–75 days) and can be dried in clusters on the compact plant for ornamental as well as culinary use; heat is typical cayenne range (70,000–85,000 SHU).

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost at 75–85°F; transplant after last frost into warm, well-drained soil; Ring of Fire is compact enough for containers (5-gallon minimum) and makes an excellent patio pepper both for culinary use and as an ornamental with upright clusters of ripening red fruits. Ring of Fire is an All-America Selections (AAS) award-winning cayenne pepper introduced by Park Seed that distinguished itself in AAS trials for its early maturity and compact plant habit compared to standard Long Red Cayenne types. Where standard Long Red Cayenne reaches 24–36 inches and takes 70–80 days from transplant, Ring of Fire is more compact (18–24 inches) and earlier (60–75 days), making it better suited for containers, shorter growing seasons, and gardeners who want cayenne peppers without managing large, sprawling plants. The fruits are classic cayenne in form — pencil-thin, 4–5 inches long, tapering to a point — and ripen from green through yellow to red in an attractive cluster that gives the variety its name; fruits remain attached to the plant well through ripening, making the loaded plant highly ornamental. Heat level is typical cayenne range (70,000–85,000 SHU) — significantly hotter than jalapeños and suitable for use in all traditional cayenne applications: fresh salsa, hot sauce, cayenne powder, drying into ristras, and as a heat element in cooking. Ring of Fire is particularly well-suited to Gulf Coast home gardeners who want a reliable cayenne producer through the long warm season.

common_name: Charleston Belle Sweet Pepper (USDA Nematode-Resistant Sweet Bell)

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; Charleston Belle was developed by the USDA-ARS Vegetable Research at Charleston, South Carolina specifically to provide root-knot nematode resistance combined with excellent sweet bell pepper quality — making it one of the most important sweet bell pepper varieties for gardeners in the Gulf South and Southeast where root-knot nematodes (Meloidogyne incognita) routinely devastate standard pepper varieties.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant into warm, well-drained soil in full sun after last frost; Charleston Belle is particularly important for Gulf South gardeners with heavy, sandy soils where root-knot nematodes are endemic — standard sweet bell varieties often show severe stunting and yield loss in nematode-infested soils where Charleston Belle performs normally. Charleston Belle was developed by USDA-ARS researchers at the U.S. Vegetable Laboratory in Charleston, South Carolina specifically to address one of the most significant problems in Southern pepper production: root-knot nematodes (primarily Meloidogyne incognita and M. arenaria), soil-dwelling parasites that infest the roots of peppers and virtually every other vegetable grown in the sandy soils of the Gulf Coast and Southeast, causing severe root galling, stunted growth, and dramatically reduced yields. Most commercial sweet bell pepper varieties are highly susceptible to these nematodes; Charleston Belle incorporates strong resistance derived from Capsicum chinense sources, allowing normal growth and fruit production in soils that would cripple susceptible varieties. The bell-shaped fruits are medium to large (3–4 lobes, 3–4 inches in diameter) and ripen from pale cream-yellow to red; they are crisp, sweet, and of excellent quality comparable to standard commercial bell varieties. Charleston Belle is an invaluable vegetable for Gulf South gardeners (zones 8–10) gardening in sandy or loamy soils where nematodes are a documented problem.
sources: USDA-ARS U.S. Vegetable Laboratory, Charleston SC

common_name: Carolina Wonder Sweet Pepper (NCSU Disease-Resistant Sweet Bell)

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; Carolina Wonder was developed by North Carolina State University as a disease-resistant sweet bell pepper with resistance to Phytophthora blight, bacterial leaf spot, and tobacco etch virus — disease complex that significantly limits sweet bell pepper production in humid Southeastern climates — making it one of the most reliable sweet bells for home gardeners across the Atlantic and Gulf Coast states.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant into warm, fertile, well-drained soil after last frost; Carolina Wonder's disease resistance package makes it superior to susceptible commercial bell varieties in humid climates where Phytophthora blight and bacterial leaf spot cause mid-season collapse of unprotected plants. Carolina Wonder was developed at North Carolina State University's pepper breeding program to address the specific disease challenges of Southeastern sweet bell pepper production — the combination of summer heat, humidity, rainfall, and the disease complex it creates (Phytophthora capsici root and crown rot, bacterial leaf spot caused by Xanthomonas, tobacco etch virus) creates extremely difficult conditions for standard sweet bell varieties that lack specific resistance genes. Carolina Wonder combines resistance to multiple diseases with excellent sweet bell quality: the large (4-lobed, blocky, 3.5–4 inch), thick-walled fruits are green-to-red with crisp, sweet flesh typical of the best sweet bells. The disease resistance package — particularly the Phytophthora crown and root rot resistance — gives Carolina Wonder dramatically better survival in wet years and poorly drained soils compared to susceptible varieties. Carolina Wonder is sometimes paired with Charleston Belle (which adds nematode resistance not included in Carolina Wonder) in gardens where both nematodes and Phytophthora are concerns; together they address the full disease complex of Gulf South pepper production.

common_name: Long Red Cayenne Pepper (Classic American Cayenne)

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; Long Red Cayenne is the standard, classic cayenne pepper variety — a long-season heirloom producing pencil-thin, wrinkled, 6–8 inch red pods with 30,000–50,000 SHU heat, the benchmark from which all other cayenne peppers are measured and the source of commercial cayenne powder, hot sauce, and the classic Louisiana-style hot sauce heat.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant into warm, well-drained, fertile soil; Long Red Cayenne is a vigorous grower that benefits from staking when loaded with fruit; pick all red and drying fruits promptly to encourage continued production through the season. Long Red Cayenne is the classic cayenne pepper — an old heirloom open-pollinated variety that represents the original 'cayenne' type from which the broad category of cayenne peppers takes its name (likely from Cayenne, French Guiana, where related chiles were grown). The fruits are distinctive: pencil-thin (1/2 to 3/4 inch in diameter), 6–8 inches long, wrinkled and irregular in cross-section, tapering to a point, and ripening to a bright red — characteristics that make Long Red Cayenne immediately identifiable and distinct from the smooth-skinned, shorter 'Ring of Fire' type. The heat level of 30,000–50,000 SHU is moderate-hot — significantly hotter than jalapeños but substantially milder than habaneros — in the range that defines 'cayenne heat' in American cuisine. Long Red Cayenne is the source (via cultivated strains) of commercial cayenne powder, an essential spice in Cajun and Creole cooking, Indian cuisine, and most dry spice blends; of Louisiana-style hot sauce; of the dried cayenne pods used in Chinese and Sichuan cooking; and of the classic sriracha-style sauces. Prolific production on vigorous plants (24–36 inches) in warm climates; excellent for drying on the plant or on strings.

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; Lipstick is an early-maturing pimento-type sweet pepper producing small, conical, bright red fruits in 53–65 days from transplant — earlier than most sweet bells — with very thick walls, exceptional sweetness, and a unique, deeply sweet pimento flavor that makes them excellent eaten fresh from the plant; the compact plants and early ripening make Lipstick one of the most productive sweet peppers for northern gardens and containers.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant into warm, well-drained soil after last frost; Lipstick is compact enough for containers (5-gallon or larger) and produces more reliably in cooler summers than large-fruited bell types; harvest when fully red for maximum sweetness or use green for a more traditional bell pepper flavor. Lipstick is an early-maturing pimento-type sweet pepper that has become one of the most popular sweet peppers for northern gardens and container cultivation because of its reliable, early production in climates where large-fruited bell varieties may not ripen consistently before fall frost. The small to medium, conical (pointed at the bottom), bright red fruits (2–3 inches long, 2 inches wide) are shaped like a traditional pimento or piquillo pepper rather than a large, blocky bell; they have very thick walls and exceptionally sweet, rich pimento-style flavor that is considered by many to be superior in eating quality to standard bell peppers when eaten raw or roasted. At 53–65 days from transplant, Lipstick ripens 2–3 weeks ahead of most bell varieties — a significant advantage in zones 4–6 where summer is compressed — and the compact plants (18–24 inches) are manageable in containers or small garden beds. Lipstick is also recommended for Gulf Coast gardens as an early-season sweet pepper that produces reliably before summer heat reduces fruit set.
